item.title: Why GTA V is the best satire for the capitalist economy
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
Why GTA V is the best satire for the capitalist economy

" You know, Devin, the way I see it – and hey, I’m no intelligent businessman like you, but the way I see it – there’s two great evils that bedevil American capitalism of the type that you practice. Number one is outsourcing. You paid a private company to do your dirty work for ya, and then you underpaid that company because you thought you were big enough and bad enough that you didn’t have to play by the rules. “
item.content:
Why GTA V is the best satire for the capitalist economy

" You know, Devin, the way I see it – and hey, I’m no intelligent businessman like you, but the way I see it – there’s two great evils that bedevil American capitalism of the type that you practice. Number one is outsourcing. You paid a private company to do your dirty work for ya, and then you underpaid that company because you thought you were big enough and bad enough that you didn’t have to play by the rules. “

The Grand Theft Auto franchise has always struck me beacuse of the incredible storytelling, but most importantly because of the immersion, satire and social critique of the American society. In this article I want to analyze how GTA V, a 13 years old game is able to create a perfect immersion with its dark satire.

Radio and TV commercials

One of the best things about every Grand Theft Auto game is the carefully curated radio music selection, but this is not the point of this article, instead I want to focus more into the advertisement intermissions, I believe they are a great way to make Los Santos and Blaine County more immersive by introducing you to fictional brands that you can recognize everywhere in the game.

One thing that I really like is that in the real world those commercials try to sell you the product by lying and deceiving you by showing you frivolous and superficial features and hiding all of the defects and making you believe that it’s “the next big thing” and you have to have it, but in game the commercials use the same sensationalistic tones while presenting the advertised product in a brutally honest way (while making fun of you, the consumer). Let’s take for example one of the beer brands in the game, Pisswasser, it literally means “Piss water” in German!

We can make the same reasoning about the in game tv, but in this case the “exagerated” effect is 100 times stronger, we can look at this commercial for “Ammu-Nation”, the weapons store, that promotes a completa apocalypse kit, complete with pornography, birth control pills and “cyanide to go out like a man”.

Going back to the radio, another thing I really appreciate both for the immersion and for the social critique are the news intermissions from “Weazel News” (parody of Fox News), whose tagline is “Confirming your prejudices”, this is a strong remark on how mass medias can be manipulated to shift the public opinion.

Here are some of most hilarious news I found:

  • “Web juggernaut Eyefind is sending cars with sophisticated cameras to take pictures of every street and house in America, including images of the inside of your home. CEO Trip Hammer says that having every home and its contents searchable is the future. “This is the future!” Privacy rights advocates were booed by liberal activists who feel everything should be online, free, and searchable.” This is an obvious critique of Google and how its product keep eroding out privacy day after day.
  • “Los Santos residents are excited at the announcement of a contest between Los Santos and Beijing for the title of world’s most polluted city. “We’re gonna win this one! China doesn’t stand a chance! C’mon, Los Santos, let’s show ‘em America don’t back down! Nor recycle!” I believe this news piece is more important than ever after Trump decided to exit the Paris climate agreement, and I really like how it mocks climate change deniers.

Brands

I briefly talked about some product brands in the previous paragraph but I want to talk more about them. First of all I believe that Rockstar made a genious move, by creating fictional car brands they avoided costly licensing fees while still recreating realistic counter parts to the IRL cars. (They even got noticed by the Renault UK Twitter acccount).

Let’s see some comparisons between the in game brand and the IRL one:

  • eCola is the in game counterpart to Coca Cola, the name alludes to the E-Coli bacteria and the company’s slogan “Deliciously infectious” suggests that it is higly addictive, just like the real beverage
  • Taco Bomb is the in game counterpart to Taco Bell, the “Bomb” in the name is a reference to the explosive diarrhea you will have after eating there
  • Übermacht is the counterpart to BMW, Übermacht in german means “superior”, a reference to the stereotypical BWM owner that feels like he owns the road
  • Enus is the counterpart Rolls Royce and Bentley, you can see the play on the word “Anus”, same as another car brand Annis
  • LifeInvader is the counterpart to Facebook, the name mocks Facebook lack of privacy; furthermore the analogous to a “like” is a “stalk” on the LifeInvader website.

Websites and the stock market

Another cool thing that was added by Rockstar since GTA IV is the ability to browse the web, and in GTA V it is just as crazy as the IRL internet, but for me one of the coolest thing you can do is trading stocks.

There are two stock markets, the “BAWSAQ” (it literally spells “ballsack”), a parody of NASDAQ and the “LCN” a parody for NYSE (New York Stock Exchange) and they both trade various brands from the GTA universe, the crucial part is that they reflect what happens in the game world.

When we are introduced to Lester, the mastermind behind all of the heists, he tasks Micheal to substitute a demo Lifeinvader device with another one rigged with explosives, this device will be used by Jay Norris (Lifeinvader’s CEO) during a keynote and it will kill him on live television. After this mission Lester mentions how he did this just so he could do some insider trading, we basically helped him to manipulate the market, but this is just the first time this is going to happen. Later down the story Franklin will be tasked with various assassination missions, here are a few notable ones:

  • during the hotel assassination mission we are tasked to kill the CEO of “Bilkinton Research”, the company released a pill to cure erectile dysfunction but it was found that it is the cause of various heart attacks and the CEO is tryng to cover up the scandal. This is a critique of the pharmaceutical industry where shareholder value is treated with more importance than a patient’s wellbeing
  • during the multi target assassitation we are tasked to kill four corrupt jurors bribed by Redwood Cigarettes to rig a class action lawsuit related to lung disease, this is a critique on how the tobacco industry has been trying to cover up the damages done by its product by first of all lying about the potential risks of tobacco smoking and then by corrupting judges.

Cults

Lastly I want to explore the three main cults inside the game, one for each charachter

Children of the Mountain

The Children of the Mountain is a religious movement based in Strawberry that can be joined by Franklin, he will receive an e-mail telling him to visit their website where he will answer a multiple choiche test but regardless of the answers the result will be the same, a message will appear telling him that he is frustrated with his life and he needs to find a deeper purpose in his life. After completing the test there are other stages to progress in the cult and to complete them Franklin will have to donate money on the cult’s website. When all the donations are made a C.o.M branded t-shirt will appear in Franklin’s wardrobe with a price point of fifteen thousand dollars (that’s how much he donated). This cult is the one we know the least about and with the least activities required to end the story line, but according to them this is not a cult but it’s actually all about self-actualization.

The Children of Mountain movement is a satire for the whole self-help industry and life coaching business, these figures try and sell you super expensive courses with the promise of “unlocking your true potential”. Also it’s not a coincidence that this cult is exclusive to Frankiln, he is the embodiment of hustle culture, he constantly obsesses on how to get out of the ghetto and the cult, just like those self-help gurus, try to leverage this to make money off of those people.

The Altruists

The Altruists is a cult that is located in the Chiliad Mountain State Wilderness and you can interact with it when playing with Trevor. This cult is made up of baby boomers that believe that technology and every generation after them is the root of all problems, most of them can be see naked inside their camp. Trevor can interact with them by bringing lost hitchikers to the camp, it’s implied that it’s for cannibalism. After four people are sent to the camp, an event is triggered in which Trevor is being taken to the camp at gunpoint and he has to fight his way trough the exit.

The cult is heavily inspired by the Unabomber, infact if we decode the morse code text inside their website we can see the message “THE INDUSTRIAL REVOLUTION HAS BEEN A DISASTER TO THE STABILITY OF THE HUMAN RACE” and it’s very similar to the opening line of his manifesto. The fact that this cult literally cannibalize young people is a metaphore for how the older generations have been and are destroying the younger generations’ future. The Altruists can be seen as an extremization of the mentality that many baby boomers have, they think that everything was better before, and they believe so much in this philosophy that they are willing to live in a camp without any modern technology that is almost completely separated from the modern outside world.

The Epsilon Program

The last cult we are going to talk about is my favorite one, The Epsilon Program and it’s exclusive to Micheal. While its biggest appearance is in GTA V, the cult was introduced way back in GTA San Andreas.

Led by Cris Formage, the cult’s lore revolves around completely absurd doctrines: such as the Earth being exactly 157 years old, dinosaurs being a lie, and humanity descending from an alien emperor named Kraff. The actions we are forced to do as Micheal are probably the ones that will take the most to do, as with the Children of the Mountain cult, Micheal is forced to make a lot of donations, but unlike the previous cult you have to perform other tasks such as running five miles in the desert, stealing cars for the cult and wearing a weird baby blue robe for days. It all ends in a final mission where Michael must escort the cult’s massive cash reserves, the game leaves you with two choices: stay loyal and receive a rusty tractor as your “spiritual reward”, or gun down the cult members and walk away with $2.1 million in stolen cash.

Apart from the ones I told you before, I there are another two differences with the Children of the Mountain, first of all this cult is a lot more popular, it’s marketed everywhere from billboards, to celebrities on tv and radio advertisments to the point that its logo is inside the Los Santos badge (along with the bigfoot); the second difference is that just like many cults (for example Scientology, the cult that Epsilonism makes fun of) it is being marketed to actors and VIPs mainly so that they can make more money from rich people.

This is a textbook parody of Scientology, you can see this starting with the sci-fi doctrines, epsilonists believe the world was created by Kraff and this is a jab at scientologist texts regarding Xenu and the “Galactic Confederacy”. Another way we can see how the Epsilon program mimics Scientology is the fact that like Scientology owns many centers in LA to attract actors and celebrities, the Epsilon Program targets the Vinewood elite by offering them exclusive networking meetings disguised as indoctrination.

Final thoughts

I have always been a fan of the world Rockstar Games created, with its incredible realism and immersion and with the current state of the world I thought that this is the best time to write another article after a long time.

Since I am not a native English speaker I used AI to help me write small sentences and translations but I tried to limit its use and most importantly to not use it when expressing my beliefs.

The image I have used in the front cover is a screenshot from this GTA Series video and a lot of infos have been taken from the GTA Wiki

I hope you enjoyed this article, if you have something to share about this you can use this repository’ issue tab.

item.title: Building a Santa tracker
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
Building a Santa tracker

Introduction

Since I was a little kid I’ve always been very interested in computers and electronics. The very first computer I had was a really crappy laptop that couldn’t play any videogames so the only thing I could do on it was browsing the web, and on Christmas Eve I was always greeted by Christmas themed Google Doodles, these little things always felt special, especially during Christmas since they always showed a map of Santa Claus movements. Today I will explain the logic behind the Santa tracker and we will build together a small Python script that shows Santa’s movements in real time.
item.content:
Building a Santa tracker

Introduction

Since I was a little kid I’ve always been very interested in computers and electronics. The very first computer I had was a really crappy laptop that couldn’t play any videogames so the only thing I could do on it was browsing the web, and on Christmas Eve I was always greeted by Christmas themed Google Doodles, these little things always felt special, especially during Christmas since they always showed a map of Santa Claus movements. Today I will explain the logic behind the Santa tracker and we will build together a small Python script that shows Santa’s movements in real time.

Reverse-engineering time

The first thing I did was searching for a Wikipedia article about it, and I actually found it! The crazy thing I found out is that the code for the website is on Github.

I looked into the repository and I found a firebase.js with some configuration settings, from there I decided that it was much easier to use the browser devtools and just look for network traffic from Firebase.

I found this URL that looked promising:

https://firebasestorage.googleapis.com/v0/b/santa-api.appspot.com/o/route-2025%2Fsanta_en.json?2021=&alt=media

The URL returns an enormous JSON file, that actually contains a list of coordinates and timestamps with various other informations, I actually found the route!

To be honest I initially thought that the logic behind this applications was a little more complex, since you don’t want a kid to see that Santa is over his house at 2:00 PM on Christmas Eve. The easy fix to this problem is just careful route planning, the developers just needed to input a route that matched the new day line as it moved so that Santa would be in the right place at the exact time.

Building our tracker

Decoding the JSON

The JSON file is just a big list of objects, let’s see the one for Rome:

"id": "rome", // the object id is just the city name in lowercase
"arrival": 1577226180000, // time of arrival in unix timestamp
"departure": 1577226240000, // time of departure in unix timestamp
"population": 2318895, // city population
"presentsDelivered": 4761568882, // counter for presents delivered
"city": "Rome", // name of the city
"region": "Italy", 
"location": { // coordinates
    "lat": 41.895466,
    "lng": 12.482324
},
"details": // this is a list of pictures and google maps links, we won't need these

The structure is pretty simple, the only problem I saw is that those timestamps are stuck in 2019, so we will need to account for this in the script.

Since it would be really wasteful to make a request to that URL every time we are going to run our script, we can download the JSON file and put it in the same directory as the script.

Let’s now start writing the program, first of all we will need a couple of imports from the Python standard library:

import json
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone

We need the json library to decode the JSON file and the time and datetime libraries to get the current time and do some “space-time bending” stuff to account for the mismatching date in the JSON.

To make the code a bit more readable we can define two constants:

JSON_TIMESTAMP_YEAR = 2019
FILENAME = "route.json"

JSON_TIMESTAMP_YEAR actually matches the year of the timestamps inside the JSON, and the FILENAME constant is just the path for the JSON file.

Now we are going to write a function that will change the current UNIX epoch timestamp, so that the time (hours, minutes and seconds) is the same but the date will be Christmas eve of 2019:

def get_christmas_eve_timestamp(year: int) -> int:
    now_utc = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
    target_date = now_utc.replace(year=year, month=12, day=24)
    return int(target_date.timestamp()) * 1000

You may notice in the last line that we multiply the timestamp times 1000, that’s because the .timestamp() method returns a float with the integer part equals to the number of seconds since January 1st 1970 and the real part is the amount of milliseconds that passed since that date, and the JSON file actually includes the milliseconds in the integer part of the timestamp.

Note: from now on I will refer to the current timestamp as the return value from this function.

Of course we need to parse the JSON file, so here is another helper function to do just that:

def read_route_json() -> list:
    with open(FILENAME, "r") as file:
        dict = json.load(file)
        return dict["destinations"]

We parse the document with json.load() and then we return the only entry in the file, the destinations list.

With these two helper functions out of the way we can focus on the logic of this application, during the lifetime of the application we can have 4 cases:

  • Santa has not started his trip yet, so the current timestamp is lower than the first arrival timestamp
  • Santa is delivering gifts to a certain city, so the current timestamp is in range of one of the JSON object’s arrival and departure timestamp
  • Santa is moving between cities, the current timestamp is in range of a object’s departure timestamp and the the next object’s arrival timestamp
  • Santa has finished his trip, the current timestamp is greater than the last object’s departure timestamp

So with that in mind we can write a function that will return a string with Santa’s status given the route and the current timestamp:

def get_santa_status(current_time, route) -> str:
    # Case 1: Santa hasn't started
    if current_time < route[0]["arrival"]:
        return "Santa is sleeping at the North Pole."

    for i in range(len(route)):
        stop = route[i]

        # Case 2: Santa is delivering present at a city
        if stop["arrival"] <= current_time <= stop["departure"]:
            return f"🎁 DELIVERING PRESENTS! Location: {stop['city']} ({stop['region']}) ({stop['location']['lat']}, {stop['location']['lng']})"

        # if statement needed to avoid overflow
        if i < len(route) - 1:
            next_stop = route[i + 1]
            # Case 3: Santa is travelling
            if stop["departure"] < current_time < next_stop["arrival"]:
                return f"🛷 FLYING! From {stop['city']} ({stop['region']}) -> to {next_stop['city']} ({next_stop['region']})\n"

    # Case 4: Santa has finished
    return "🎅 Santa has finished his run! Back at North Pole."

Finally we need to put everything together, we will have a loop that checks for updates every 30 seconds, since from what I saw the minimum distance between timestamps is 1 minute:

def main():
    route_data = read_route_json()

    try:
        # clear screen with ANSI codes:
        print("\033[H\033[J", end="")
        while True:
            current_time = get_christmas_eve_timestamp(JSON_TIMESTAMP_YEAR)

            status = get_santa_status(current_time, route_data)
            print(status)

            time.sleep(30)

            # clear screen with ANSI codes:
            print("\033[H\033[J", end="")

    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        print("Tracking stopped.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

To make the UI a bit cleaner I added those two print statement with seemingly garbage strings, those are ANSI codes and are interpreted by the terminal as escape sequences to clear the screen, and the try-except statement so that when the user stops the execution with Ctrl+C we don’t show an error but just an exit message.

If you want to just see how it works without copying the code from here, this is the project repository, you just need to clone it and run the script with python main.py.

Conclusions

The boredom of the holidays, with the help of memories from my childhood gave me the idea to understand how Google’s Santa Tracker works, honestly it’s been really fun building this small program and I hope that reading this article will give you the same enjoyment that I had while writing it.

Merry Christmas and Happy New Year!

Sources

  • Google Santa Tracker on Wikipedia
  • santa-tracker-web on Github

item.title: JWT 101
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
JWT 101

JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, URL-safe means of representing
claims to be transferred between two parties.  The claims in a JWT
are encoded as a JSON object that is used as the payload of a JSON
Web Signature (JWS) structure or as the plaintext of a JSON Web
Encryption (JWE) structure, enabling the claims to be digitally
signed or integrity protected with a Message Authentication Code
(MAC) and/or encrypted.

This is the abstract of RFC 7519, the document that brought JSON Web Tokens to life. There is a lot to unpack in this paragraph so i will explain it in a simpler way:
item.content:
JWT 101

JSON Web Token (JWT) is a compact, URL-safe means of representing
claims to be transferred between two parties.  The claims in a JWT
are encoded as a JSON object that is used as the payload of a JSON
Web Signature (JWS) structure or as the plaintext of a JSON Web
Encryption (JWE) structure, enabling the claims to be digitally
signed or integrity protected with a Message Authentication Code
(MAC) and/or encrypted.

This is the abstract of RFC 7519, the document that brought JSON Web Tokens to life. There is a lot to unpack in this paragraph so i will explain it in a simpler way:

A JWT is a compact way of representing data that need to be transferred between two parties, the data is encoded in JSON format and it either digitally signed (with a secret code for symmetric algorithms like HMAC256) or encrypted (with a private key for asymmetric algorithms like RSA).

The signature is used to prove two things:

  • integrity: it proves that the token has not been tampered with, even if a single character has been altered then the signature attached to the token and the signature calculated would not match
  • authenticity: it proves that the token has really been sent from who it claims to be sent from, this is achieved by the fact that only the server knows the secret code so nobody else can calculate a new valid signature

If this sounds too complicated I’ll try and explain it with more details next, right now the only thing you need to know is that a JWT is just a fancy JSON object used for information exchange or user authentication.

Structure

A raw JWT is formed like this:

xxxxx.yyyyy.zzzzz

There are three Base64 encoded parts separated by a dot:

  • header
  • payload
  • signature

Header

The header is typically made up of two parts:

  • the token type, so JWT
  • the algorithm used for signature or encryption, for example HMAC SHA256 or RSA

Here is an example of a JWT header:

{
  "alg": "HS256",
  "typ": "JWT"
}

This JSON object is then Base64 encoded and it is placed as the first field of the JWT.

Payload

The payload is the second part of the JWT and it contains the claims, claims are just informations about the user, there are three different type of claims:

  • registered claims: these claims are predifined by RFC 7519, they are not mandatory but recommended, they can be things like exp, the timestamp of token expiry or sub, the subject of this token
  • public claims: these can be defined by whoever is using the JWT, but to avoid collision they should be registered to the IANA JWT registry to avoid collisions
  • private claims: these are custom claims and they are not registered nor public claims

The minimal example of what a JWT payload should contain looks like this:

{
    "exp": 1766314986,
    "sub": "user:2315"
}

The expiry timestamp is really important because when a request with an expired JWT is sent to the server, it will generate a new one. This mechanism is needed to make sure that the attack window for a malicious third party is very short and that even if he was able to intercept a token he wouldn’t have the time to use it.

The JSON object containing the payload is Base64 encoded and added as the second field in the token.

Signature

To create a signature we just need the header, payload and secret code and calculate the digital signature of that string, if we use HMAC SHA256 we would do this:

HMACSHA256(base64UrlEncode(header) + "." + base64UrlEncode(payload), secret)

Use

Let’s now see the authentication flow for a web service that uses JWTs:

  1. Client logs in with username and password
  2. The server generates the header, payload and signature
  3. The token is sent to the client, he will now store the token somewhere safe. Now that the client has aquired the JWT he can use it to authenticate himself.
  4. The client sends a request to an endpoint that needs authentication, in the HTTP request, the token will be added to the Authorization: Bearer <token> header
  5. The server receives the request and the token
  6. The server calculates the token signature and checks if the two signatures (the one sent along with the token and the one calculated right now) match, if they don’t an error is sent to the client
  7. The token is decoded by the server, if it has an expiry timestamp, the server checks if it has expired; if it has then an error is sent to the client
  8. The client has been authenticated, the request is processed and a response is sent to the client

Conclusions

This article touches the fundamentals about token based authentication, I wanted to write this article since I have been implementing authentication for a new REST-API I’m building at the moment, it isn’t a very big project and there is no sensitive data exchanged but I still wanted to build something that uses the technologies commonly used in more important and bigger projects, I will give you an update when the first version of this project is out!

Sources

  • RFC 7519
  • jwt.io, this is a cool website to thinker around and learn how these token work in practice

item.title: How to handle exceptions in Java
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
Exceptions in Java

What could possibly go wrong?

In the lifetime of an application not everything will go as planned, mistakes will be made both from the user and the programmer but sometimes you will just be unlucky (a random bit flip caused by a cosmic ray will change your game state… and give a speedrunner a huge timesave)

We can categorize the sources of error in two groups:

  • internal errors, for example the user wants to do a division by zero or a method is called on a null reference
  • external errors, for example a comunication error with a remote DBMS or even an error inside the JVM, for example an “Out of memory error”

The OOP way of handling those problems is by handling them outside the normal control flow.
item.content:
Exceptions in Java

What could possibly go wrong?

In the lifetime of an application not everything will go as planned, mistakes will be made both from the user and the programmer but sometimes you will just be unlucky (a random bit flip caused by a cosmic ray will change your game state… and give a speedrunner a huge timesave)

We can categorize the sources of error in two groups:

  • internal errors, for example the user wants to do a division by zero or a method is called on a null reference
  • external errors, for example a comunication error with a remote DBMS or even an error inside the JVM, for example an “Out of memory error”

The OOP way of handling those problems is by handling them outside the normal control flow.

What even is an exception?

Like everything in Java an exception is an Object that is used to tell the caller method that there is a problem and it needs to be handled.

Here is what happens in detail

  • the callee method checks conditions that can create errors
  • it throws an exception if these conditions are true
  • the caller now has to handle the exception

Let’s see an example:

public float division(int a, int b){
    if (b == 0){
        throw new IllegalArgumentException("Division by zero");
    }

    return ((float) a )/ b;
}

In this case if the divisor b is zero then an exception is thrown, now the method execution stops and we return to the caller, it can do three things:

  • prevent the exception: the caller can check beforehand if b == 0, in this case the exception will not be thrown, if the caller cannot do these checks (because it would be too complex) it can do two things:
    • it can rethrow and propagate the exception to its caller because it does not have enough context or responsibility and it doesn’t know how to handle it
    • it can catch the exception with a try-catch block and handle it

You should always try to prevent the exception, because handling exceptions can be computationally expensive and since exceptions do not follow the usual control flow it is harder to program.

The try-catch block

As I said we can handle the exception by using a try-catch block, let’s see an example:

public void tryCatch(){
    try{
        int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
        System.out.println(arr[4]);
    } 
    catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e){
        System.out.println("Caught an array index error: ");
    }
    catch (Exception e){
        System.out.println("Caught a generic exception");
    }
}

We wrap the code that could throw an exception (in this simple example an exception will surely be thrown) in the try block, if an exception is actually thrown then the execution of the try block stops and the code inside the first catch statement that matches the exception will be executed.

The order of the catch blocks matters, if we want a detailed explanation of what happened we need to put the most generic exception type at the bottom (in this case we put the base class Exception at the bottom) because otherwise the thrown exception will always be caught with the most generic error message.

Checked and unchecked exceptions

There are two big families of exceptions, checked exceptions and unchecked exceptions:

  • unchecked exceptions are tipically the result of interal errors and they are often avoidable if the programmer pays attention, also we are not obligated by the compiler to handle them
  • checked exceptions are the result of external errors and the compiler forces us to handle them (the compiler “checks” for unhandled exceptions), they are a great tool during debugging but in a production environment they shouldn’t be ever thrown or caught in a try-catch block, since they are the result of a programmer error.

We can distinguish them by looking at their superclass, checked exceptions extend Exception while unchecked exceptions extend RuntimeException.

When we throw a checked exception we need to to three things to avoid being screamed at by the compiler:

  • the exception must be declared in the called method header with the throws clause
public void close() throws IOException
  • the caller cannot ingore this exception so it either need to handle it with a try-catch block or it needs to rethrow it

(NOTE: the throws clause is compulsory for checked exceptions but it can be also used with unchecked exceptions, in this case though the compiler will not do any checks)

Gracefully restore external resources

After using external resources (connection to DBMS or access to a file) we need to restore them to a normal state even when exceptions are thrown, to do this we use the finally block.

The finally block is placed after the last catch block and it is always executed:

  • it is executed if inside the try block no exceptions are thrown
  • it is executed if inside the try block an exception is thrown and it is caught
  • it is executed if inside the try block an exception is thrown and it is uncaught

Sometimes exception can be thrown even inside a finally block, for example when we are closing a file:

finally{
    try{
        if (file != null){
            file.close()
        }
    } catch (IOException e){
        System.out.println(e.printStackTrace());
    }
}

in this case there is nothing we can do, we tried our best but we couldn’t restore the resource to a normal state.

From Java 8 onwards a new construct, try-with-resources has been added, this can be used with classes that implement AutoClosable, let’s see an example:

try (FileWriter f = new FileWriter("text.txt")){
    f.write("foo");
} catch (IOException e){
    System.out.println(e.printStackTrace());
}

when this code is run, the expression inside the parenthesis is executed before the try block, if there is an IOException the catch block will handle it otherwise we just write “foo” to the file; in any case since FileWriter implements AutoClosable, the close() method will be called and any exception thrown by it will be handled automatically without using finally.

item.title: Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?

Before diving in our “investigation”, I wanted to tell you how this article came to life…

A few weeks ago I was cramming for my computer architecture exam and I wanted some kind of background music that was not too distracting, you might say the obvious pick would be some lo-fi hip-hop radio playlist but for me it’s way too chill and relaxing (not ideal when you are sleep deprived), so I decided to pick this synthwave playlist that I used to listen to and after a few songs an inexplicable wave of nostalgia and melanchony hit me, that’s when I started to search why is this genre so nostalgic.
item.content:
Why does synthwave make us nostalgic?

Before diving in our “investigation”, I wanted to tell you how this article came to life…

A few weeks ago I was cramming for my computer architecture exam and I wanted some kind of background music that was not too distracting, you might say the obvious pick would be some lo-fi hip-hop radio playlist but for me it’s way too chill and relaxing (not ideal when you are sleep deprived), so I decided to pick this synthwave playlist that I used to listen to and after a few songs an inexplicable wave of nostalgia and melanchony hit me, that’s when I started to search why is this genre so nostalgic.

What is synthwave?

Despite being heavily inspired by the 80s, synthwave is a relatively new genre, it was born from the minds of artists from the French electronic scene in the late 2000s, as the name suggests the genre is centered around the heavy use of analog synths and usually has a quite energetic rhythm, it is also quite similar to French house music (the only good thing France created \s) and Italo disco (the great Giorgio Moroder is one of the fathers of electronic music, which synthwave is a subgenre of)

Synthwave in pop culture

One of the things that brought synthwave to the mainstream was the movie Drive (Ryan Gosling is literally me), and its soundtrack featuring “Kavinsky”’s famous “Nightcall” and “College”’s “A Real Hero”, the film’s theme song

Another very important movie that is associated with synthwave is Blade Runner 2049, but in this case I think that people associate this movie more with the visual aesthetic of synthwave rather than the music.

The visual aesthetic

I’m sure you have all seen some pictures (like the one on header of this article) that follow the synthwave aesthetic, they all have something in common:

  • bright purple neon lights
  • futuristic cars and wirefame graphics
  • sunsets (a lot of sunsets)
  • infinite landscapes (like tall skyscraper but also mountain terrains)

And I think that the visuals also contribute to the general nostalgia feeling, for me it’s mostly the sunsets but also the infinite landscapes, they are mostly empty, there isn’t a single person and it gives off the same vibe as a liminal space.

Nostalgia for something I never lived

Let’s now try and understand where this nostalgia stems from…

As I said this music is heavily inspired from the 80s but there is a problem, I was born in the 2000s, how can I feel nostalgic about it?

The thing is I have only consumed media from the 80s sporadically and I really don’t know much about that time period so really there has to be another reason why this music makes me nostalgic…

So the only things that I think can explain this nostalgic feeling are:

  • the use of warm sounding analog synths and recording equipment, along with other effects create a kind of “past memory effect”, they make the music seem like a past memory, fuzzy and not quite clear
  • specific chord progressions that trigger this melanchony, I know that specific chords and keys can be used to bring a certain feeling to the listener but there are a couple of problems:
    • first of all there are countless synthwave songs and they all use different keys
    • then since it is electronic music, traditional chord progressions and rhythmic patterns are not always followed
    • even if a song uses particular chord progressions I have not found any specific chord that brings this sense of nostalgia, so this is a dead end

I’m going to be frank with you, I have no idea why this music brings me this melancholy and nostalgia for the good times (in my case the 2010s), and I really hope to find an answer to this someday.

In the end, synthwave is not just a genre, it’s a time machine. Whether it takes us to the 80s or to a more recent past, if we bring its glorious synth melodies and funky rhythm close to our heart it will remind us that past memories are still with us, and that the good times we miss and cherish so much are yet to come.

item.title: Conditional logic in MIPS assembly
item.author: enrico@example.com (EnricoBDev)
item.description:
Conditional logic in MIPS assembly

To build traditional conditional logic the language offers us some control flow instructions and some conditional data transfer instructions, let’s see them

Mnemonic Sintax Meaning
beq (branch equal) beq $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are equal
bne (branch not equal)* bne $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are different
slt (set on less than)* slt $s0 $t0 $t1 Sets register $s0 to 1 if $t0 < $t1
j (jump) jump Label Jumps to Label

* there is also another variant of this instruction that compares the content of a register and an inmmediate value (slti)
item.content:
Conditional logic in MIPS assembly

To build traditional conditional logic the language offers us some control flow instructions and some conditional data transfer instructions, let’s see them

Mnemonic Sintax Meaning
beq (branch equal) beq $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are equal
bne (branch not equal)* bne $t0 $t1 Label Jumps to “Label” if $t0 and $t1 are different
slt (set on less than)* slt $s0 $t0 $t1 Sets register $s0 to 1 if $t0 < $t1
j (jump) jump Label Jumps to Label

* there is also another variant of this instruction that compares the content of a register and an inmmediate value (slti)

Now we will see every condition present in any modern programming language, in each example we will translate the same if statement, but we will change the condition, here is the C code we will translate:

if (f condition g){
    f +=1;
} else {
    f -= 1;
}

We will also suppose that the variable f is the $t0 register and g is $t1

!= and == condition

To create these conditions we just need to use the provided bne, beq instructions, let’s see an example with the != condition:

beq $t0, $t1, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

We can see that in this example we actually use the opposite instruction (we are using beq for a != condition) but it still works, since we jump to the else label if the two variables are equal, otherwise we continue with the block inside the if statement.

>= condition

To make this condition we are going to use the slt instruction and we are going to use the same trick as before, where we negate the condition and jump to Else if the negated condition is true, let’s see:

slt $s0, $t0, $t1
bne $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

slt $s0, $t0, $t1 is the most important line, it exploits the fact that the negated condition of < is >=, so if the condition we want to achieve is true slt sets $s0 = 0, then after that we use the trick i just told you in the previous paragraph where we jump to Else if the negated condition is true.

< condition

To obtain this condition we just need to change bne to beq in the previous snippet since now if the condition is true the output from slt will be 1, after all slt checks the < condition

slt $s0, $t0, $t1
beq $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

> condition

To translate this condition we use the fact that by inverting the operands of a < condition we get the > condition, let’s see the implementation:

slt $s0, $t1, $t0
beq $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

<= condition

The last condition is <=, to translate it we exploit the fact that <= is the negation of >:

slt $s0, $t1, $t0
bne $s0, $zero, Else
addi $t0, $t0, 1
j Exit

Else:
    subi $t0, $t0, 1

Exit:

We use the same check slt $s0, $t1, $t0 that we used for > but the difference is that if the condition we want to achieve is true, then slt outputs a 0 (as we said <= is the negation of >, and we just switched the operands when using slt)

And there you have it, these are all the traditional logic conditions you are familiar with, now a couple of thoughts:

  • pseudoinstructions that do these conditions (for example bgt for >) exists, these are instructions that are translated to other more basic instructions by the assembler (that’s what the $at register is used for)
  • these translations can be quite challenging to remember (and i really hope that my explanation was not too short or confusing), but recreating them could be a good exercise to learn or practice another assembly flavour (even though the ISA may give you different instructions)

If you read all of this, thank you! If you have any questions or corrections (just keep in mind English is not my mother tongue) you can put an issue on Github
